title: Input and Output
description: Printing with System.out.print/println/printf, and reading user input with Scanner.
objectives: Tell the difference between System.out.print and System.out.println
Read different types of input using Scanner
Avoid the classic nextInt() + nextLine() mistake

sections.text: We've used System.out.println() since the Introduction lesson without formally naming it. Today we cover output properly, and add input — reading values the user types in.
A built-in object representing the standard output stream — normally your console/terminal — that Java programs write text to.

rows: Prints text with no line break after it — the next output continues on the same line
Prints text and then moves to a new line — the next output starts on a fresh line

sections.text: Sometimes you want to control exactly how a value is displayed — like how many decimal places a number shows. printf ("print formatted") uses placeholders inside a text template.

sections.title: Common printf placeholders
rows: A decimal value rounded to 2 decimal places
A platform-correct new line (preferred over \n inside printf)

sections.text: %.2f rounds to 2 decimal places for display purposes only — it does not change the underlying value stored in the variable. gpa itself is still 8.6789 after this line runs.

To read something the user types, Java provides a class called Scanner. We haven't learned classes yet, but you can use Scanner today by following this exact pattern — we'll explain what a class truly is once we reach Object-Oriented Programming.

import java.util.Scanner; and new Scanner(System.in) contain concepts not covered yet (import, new, objects). For now, treat these two lines as a fixed pattern you copy whenever you need to read input — we revisit exactly what they mean once we reach Object-Oriented Programming.

sections.title: Common Scanner methods
rows: An entire line of text, including spaces

sections.text: This is one of the most common Scanner bugs beginners hit.

nextInt() reads only the number you typed, and leaves the leftover newline character (from pressing Enter) still waiting in the input. The very next nextLine() then immediately reads that leftover empty leftover instead of waiting for new text, so it appears to be "skipped." The common fix is to add an extra sc.nextLine(); right after nextInt() to consume that leftover newline before reading a real line of text.

sections.output:
(Assuming input: Aman, then 25)
Enter your name: Aman
Enter your age: 25
Hello Aman, you are 25 years old.

sections.text: This lesson's code reads real keyboard input, so its exact output depends on what a user types — the sample output above assumes the example values shown. This eBook's editors are for reading and retyping code only, not running it.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Calling nextLine() right after nextInt() (or nextDouble()) and being confused when it seems to get skipped — add an extra sc.nextLine(); to consume the leftover newline first.
Forgetting System.out.print() (no line break) doesn't add a space or newline automatically before the next output.
Using %d for a double or %f for an int in printf — the placeholder type must match the value's actual type.
Forgetting import java.util.Scanner; at the top of the file — without it, Scanner is unrecognized.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between System.out.print and System.out.println?
questions.answer: print outputs text with no trailing line break, so the next output continues on the same line. println outputs text and then moves to a new line.

questions.question: What class is commonly used to read user input in Java?
questions.answer: Scanner, from java.util.Scanner, wrapping System.in. It provides methods like nextInt(), nextDouble(), next(), and nextLine() for reading different types of input.

questions.question: Why does nextLine() sometimes appear to be skipped after nextInt()?
questions.answer: nextInt() only consumes the numeric characters typed, leaving the newline character (from pressing Enter) unread in the input buffer. The following nextLine() call immediately reads that leftover empty newline instead of waiting for new input. Calling an extra sc.nextLine(); after nextInt() consumes that leftover newline.

questions.question: What does %.2f do in a printf format string?
questions.answer: It formats a decimal value to show exactly 2 digits after the decimal point, rounding as needed for display — it does not change the underlying stored value.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Java prints output with System.out.print (no line break), System.out.println (adds a line break), or System.out.printf for formatted output using placeholders like %s, %d, and %.2f. Input is read with Scanner, wrapping System.in, using methods like nextInt(), nextDouble(), and nextLine() for different value types. The most common Scanner gotcha is calling nextLine() right after nextInt(): the leftover newline from the number entry gets read immediately as an empty line, so an extra sc.nextLine() call is needed to consume it first.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: println = PRINT + NEW LINE. printf = PRINT + FORMATTED. Scanner's nextInt() leaves the Enter key's newline behind — always sc.nextLine(); to clean it up before reading a real line.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: What's the difference between print and println?
Which Scanner method reads an entire line including spaces?
Why does calling nextLine() right after nextInt() often cause a bug?

codeExamples.output:
(Assuming input: Aman, then 25)
Enter your name: Aman
Enter your age: 25
Hello Aman, you are 25 years old.
